gold.rationale: The article mixes real Indiana weather trends with misleading statewide framing and manipulated storm imagery. Genuine reports of a temperature drop and some tornado-related activity are overstated by presenting localized forecast data as statewide and by using an edited tornado map that exaggerates Indiana's in-state tornado footprint.

atomic_points.post_summary: Indiana experienced a sharp spring weather swing on Wednesday with rising temperatures followed by a rapid drop, severe weather including tornado reports, and a freezing night expected for Thursday.

paragraph_level: Indiana experienced a sharp spring weather swing on Wednesday characterized by a rapid temperature drop.
Severe weather reports mounted in and near Indiana, including tornado reports linked to a supercell along the Illinois warm front and another in Newton County.
The broader two-day outbreak saw a storm count nearing 600, with most reports involving wind and hail.
By late Wednesday, Indiana transitioned from warm and humid conditions to raw and much colder conditions.
A freezing night is expected for many communities with overnight lows falling mainly into the low to mid 30s statewide.
Thursday is expected to remain cooler with highs in some parts of Indiana reaching only the upper 40s.

retrieved_evidence.text: Parts of Indiana experienced a pronounced temperature swing on Wednesday, as afternoon readings climbed to around 70 degrees Fahrenheit before a marked drop later in the day, according to preliminary observations from the National Weather Service. Meteorologists said the brief warmup was associated with a passing frontal boundary and gusty winds, followed by a surge of much colder air. Residents in central and southern counties reported stepping outside in springlike conditions at midday, only to encounter falling temperatures by evening, leading officials to remind the public to monitor rapidly changing weather through the rest of the week.

Two-day storm reports nearing 600 - mostly wind and hail. Half dozen tornado reports mainly from long-lived supercell storm that originated along a warm front in IL. This storm produced several tornadoes including one in Newton Co. late Wednesday #INwx

A long-lived supercell thunderstorm tracking along a warm front in Illinois generated several tornado reports this week, including one that crossed into Newton County in northwest Indiana, according to preliminary information from weather officials. The storm cell, described by meteorologists as persistent and highly organized, developed in a volatile environment with strong wind shear. Local authorities in the affected counties reported downed trees and minor structural damage in some communities, while teams were being dispatched to survey the paths and determine the strength and exact number of tornadoes.

Severe storms tracking across Indiana during the same period led to multiple tornado reports being logged by national and regional monitoring networks. According to weather authorities, central and southern parts of the state were of particular concern, as rotating thunderstorms developed along advancing frontal boundaries. Emergency managers in Indiana followed these reports closely, coordinating with spotters, law enforcement and local media while assessing damage potential and ensuring that residents received timely warnings as the severe weather episode unfolded.

Nearly 600 storm reports were logged over a recent two-day stretch across the broader central United States, with most accounts involving strong winds and hail, according to early tallies compiled by federal severe weather monitors. Officials said the reports, which include damage to trees, power lines and some structures, came from a wide area affected by a series of fast-moving storm systems. While detailed verification is still under way, emergency management agencies in several states have been reviewing the events and assessing whether additional resources or assistance may be needed.

In Indiana, forecasts issued by local meteorologists recently highlighted a sharp reversal from unseasonably warm conditions to a night close to or below freezing. A regional overnight temperature outlook for southwestern Indiana showed many readings clustered around 30 to 33 degrees Fahrenheit as colder air swept in behind departing storms. According to officials and weather services, this influx of cold air raised concerns about freezing conditions affecting early vegetation, rural infrastructure and lingering moisture from the severe weather episode.

Back below freezing for many locations early Thursday and for the first time in 10 days a below normal day. Thursday may only top upper 40s. Today marks the first day of the calendar when 50° is the normal #INwx

Earlier in the day, Indiana experienced notably mild conditions, with high temperatures in many locations reaching into the upper 60s and lower 70s Fahrenheit. Meteorological summaries for the state described a warm, unstable air mass in place ahead of an approaching cold front, contributing to the development of thunderstorms and the risk of tornadoes. As the front moved through, temperatures in Indiana fell sharply by evening, illustrating the pronounced warm-to-cold swing that underpinned both the severe weather outbreak and the subsequent concern over freezing overnight lows.

Weather forecasters warned that much of Indiana could see temperatures fall below the freezing mark early Thursday, with daytime highs expected to reach only the upper 40s in some areas. According to the National Weather Service, the cold spell follows a period of unseasonably warm conditions and is linked to a stronger push of cooler, drier air across the region. Officials said gardeners, farmers and those with sensitive outdoor plants should take precautions against potential frost, noting that further updates and any advisories would be issued as conditions continue to develop.
3/12/26 Tonight's Forecast: Showers ending this evening. Breezy. Decreasing Clouds and Cold overnight. Lows will be in the low to mid 30s. #tristatewx #inwx #ilwx #kywx

A recent Tri-State area forecast indicated that overnight low temperatures would mainly fall into the low to mid 30s across southwestern Indiana and adjacent parts of Kentucky and Illinois. Local meteorologists said the colder readings are expected to follow a cooler air mass settling over the Ohio Valley, prompting discussions among officials about the possibility of frost in rural and low-lying locations. Residents were advised to stay informed through local broadcasts and online updates as forecasters continue to refine temperature projections for the late-night and early-morning hours.

Temperatures are falling sharply Wednesday after the 5th 70° day of the year. This is the MOST so early in a year since 2000. Temperatures are down nearly 30° since just before 8am. Add the wind and it it feels even colder. Wind chill now into the 30s #INwx

#tristatewx #inwx #ilwx #kywx 3/11/26 Midday Update: Showers and T-storms continue to fire ahead of a cold front across Southern Illinois and Southwest Indiana. Overall, the best severe threat this afternoon will be along and South of the Ohio River. Temperatures are in the 60s North to the 70s South.
